name: Dask
import_as:
import dask.dataframe as dd
import dask.array as da
summary: Parallel computing library scaling Python code from single machines to clusters, providing lazy Pandas and NumPy-compatible APIs for out-of-core computation.

sections.name: Dask DataFrame

functions.purpose: Read CSV files into a Dask DataFrame partitioned for parallel processing
Convert a Pandas DataFrame into a Dask DataFrame with specified partitions
Execute the lazy task graph and return a Pandas DataFrame
Execute and persist the result in distributed memory for repeated access

gotchas: Dask DataFrame operations are lazy and do not execute until compute() is called; missing .compute() will return a Dask object instead of actual data, causing silent downstream errors.
Column assignment in Dask DataFrames requires map_partitions() or explicit Dask-aware operations; direct column assignment like df['new'] = series is not supported and raises an error.

sections.name: Dask Array

functions.purpose: Create a Dask array from a NumPy array with specified chunk sizes
Generate a random Dask array with given shape and chunk sizes
Sum of array elements over a given axis, computed in parallel across chunks
Dot product of two Dask arrays, chunking over the contracted dimension

gotchas: Dask Array chunk sizes significantly impact performance; chunks smaller than 1MB cause excessive task overhead, while chunks larger than 100MB may exceed worker memory limits.
Mixed chunk sizes along an axis prevent certain operations from optimizing properly; use rechunk() to regularize chunk shapes before reduction operations like sum() or mean().

sections.name: Scheduler and Compute

functions.purpose: Compute multiple Dask collections simultaneously, sharing intermediate results
Decorator to convert a Python function into a lazy Dask task
Render the task graph for a Dask collection to a file or inline
Configure the default scheduler for Dask computations

gotchas: dask.delayed() does not handle complex Python objects well; passing large Pandas DataFrames or unserializable objects between delayed tasks causes serialization overhead or crashes.
Using the synchronous scheduler with dask.compute() on large workloads blocks the main thread; switch to 'threads' or 'processes' for I/O-bound work, or use a distributed Client for clusters.

sections.name: Diagnostics

functions.purpose: Property returning the URL to the Dask distributed dashboard for monitoring
Collect results from distributed futures into the local process
Submit a function to the scheduler for execution on a worker
Restart all workers, clearing their memory and state

gotchas: Calling client.gather() on large distributed results can exhaust local memory if the aggregated output exceeds the client machine's RAM; use to_csv() or write to disk on workers instead.
client.restart() clears all worker memory and cancels running computations without warning; any unpersisted intermediate results are lost and must be recomputed from scratch.
